174.intro:
Introduction

Obstructed labour is one of the most dangerous complications of the intrapartum period, accounting for a significant proportion of maternal deaths and stillbirths in India, particularly in rural and resource-limited settings. It occurs when a mechanical barrier prevents descent of the presenting part despite adequate uterine contractions. The nurse midwife must recognise its early warning signs—including Bandl’s ring and moulding +3—and initiate emergency referral without delay, as every minute lost increases the risk of uterine rupture, vesicovaginal fistula, and maternal death.

175.intro:
Introduction

Prolonged labour is a common yet preventable cause of maternal and neonatal morbidity in India, occurring when the active phase of labour extends beyond the expected duration due to abnormalities of Power, Passage, or Passenger. The partograph, mandated under the Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) protocol, is the nurse midwife’s primary tool for early detection. Timely identification and appropriate intervention—whether oxytocin augmentation or caesarean section—can prevent progression to the far more dangerous obstructed labour.
175.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Labour in which the active phase (cervical dilation ≥4 cm) lasts more than 12 hours in a primigravida or more than 8 hours in a multigravida, with cervical dilation progressing at less than 1 cm per hour as assessed on the partograph.

176.intro:
Introduction

Understanding the causes of prolonged labour is fundamental to intrapartum nursing care, as early identification of the underlying cause determines the correct management pathway. In India, where grand multiparity and malnutrition remain prevalent, causes related to the Passage (contracted pelvis) and Power (dehydration-induced uterine inertia) are particularly common. The nurse midwife who can correctly attribute prolonged labour to Power, Passage, or Passenger will avoid the catastrophic error of administering oxytocin in the presence of obstruction.
176.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Active phase labour lasting more than 12 hours (primigravida) or 8 hours (multigravida), caused by abnormalities of the three Ps — Power (uterine contractions), Passage (pelvis and soft tissues), or Passenger (fetal size or position) — resulting in cervical dilation of less than 1 cm per hour.

177.intro:
Introduction

Prolonged labour is a significant contributor to maternal exhaustion, postpartum haemorrhage, operative delivery, and perinatal asphyxia, making it a priority topic in MUHS obstetric nursing. It is defined by failure of normal progress through the active phase of labour and must be distinguished from the latent phase, which can normally last up to 20 hours. The nurse midwife plays a frontline role in monitoring progress using the partograph and escalating care when the alert line is crossed.
177.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Labour in which the active phase lasts more than 12 hours in a primigravida or 8 hours in a multigravida, with cervical dilation less than 1 cm per hour; identified by the partograph crossing the alert line.

178.intro:
Introduction

Malpresentation — any presentation other than vertex (cephalic with occiput as denominator) — complicates approximately 4–5% of deliveries at term and is a leading indication for caesarean section in India. Breech presentation is the most common type, occurring in 3–4% of term pregnancies, and carries significant risks including cord prolapse, head entrapment, and birth asphyxia. The nurse midwife must be competent in antenatal detection, understanding of management options including external cephalic version (ECV), and safe intrapartum care.
178.def:
Definition

Malpresentation: Any presentation of the fetus at the pelvic inlet other than the vertex (occipito-anterior); includes breech (buttocks/feet), face, brow, shoulder (transverse lie), and compound presentations. Breech presentation is the most common, with the fetal buttocks or lower limbs occupying the lower pole of the uterus.

179.intro:
Introduction

Breech presentation, occurring in 3–4% of term pregnancies, represents the single most common malpresentation encountered in clinical obstetric practice in India. It is associated with significant maternal and fetal risk, primarily due to the hazard of aftercoming head entrapment and cord prolapse. With ultrasonography now widely available at secondary care level, early detection and counselling regarding external cephalic version (ECV) or planned caesarean section are key responsibilities of the trained nurse midwife.
179.def:
Definition

Breech Presentation: A longitudinal lie in which the buttocks or lower limbs of the fetus occupy the lower uterine segment and present at the pelvic inlet; classified as frank breech (hips flexed, knees extended — 65%), complete breech (hips and knees flexed — 25%), and footling/incomplete breech (one or both feet presenting — 10%).

180.intro:
Introduction

Malpresentations collectively account for a disproportionately high share of operative deliveries and perinatal mortality in India, with breech being the most common and face, brow, and shoulder presentations presenting increasingly complex management challenges. The ability to detect malpresentation antenatally through abdominal examination and ultrasonography, and to understand the mode of delivery for each type, is a core MUHS nursing competency. Timely referral to a facility with surgical capacity is the single most important intervention when malpresentation is identified.
180.def:
Definition

Malpresentation: Any fetal presentation at the pelvic inlet other than vertex; common types include breech (buttocks or feet, most common), face (fully extended head, mentum as denominator), brow (partially extended, always requires caesarean section due to 13.5 cm mentovertical diameter), and transverse/shoulder lie (always caesarean section at term).

181.intro:
Introduction

Occipitoposterior (OP) position, in which the fetal occiput faces the maternal sacrum, is the most common malposition encountered in labour, complicating approximately 10–15% of vertex presentations. It is characterised by severe backache, prolonged first and second stages, and an early urge to push before full cervical dilation. While 65% rotate spontaneously to the occiput anterior position, persistent OP requires operative intervention and the nurse midwife must provide skilled support, positional management, and continuous monitoring throughout.
181.def:
Definition

Occipitoposterior (OP) Position: A malposition in which the fetal occiput (back of the head) faces the maternal sacrum (posterior), resulting in a “back-to-back” labour characterised by severe backache, slow cervical dilation, and prolonged labour; the most common malposition in vertex presentations.

182.intro:
Introduction

Contracted pelvis, in which one or more pelvic diameters are reduced below the minimum required for safe vaginal delivery, remains a significant cause of obstructed labour and maternal mortality in India, particularly among women with a history of rickets, malnutrition, or short stature. Early identification through clinical pelvimetry and ultrasound, especially at the first antenatal visit, enables appropriate birth planning. Management in the second stage requires careful trial of labour assessment or timely caesarean section to prevent catastrophic outcomes including uterine rupture and fistula formation.
182.def:
Definition

Contracted Pelvis: A pelvis in which one or more diameters are reduced below the obstetric minimum; the normal anteroposterior (AP) diameter of the inlet is 11 cm, and a diameter of <10 cm is contracted while <8 cm is absolutely contracted, requiring elective caesarean section regardless of other factors.

183.intro:
Introduction

Obstetric emergencies are sudden, life-threatening complications of pregnancy and labour that require immediate, coordinated action to prevent maternal and fetal death. In India, they remain a leading contributor to the maternal mortality ratio (MMR) of approximately 97 per 100,000 live births (SRS 2018–2020), with haemorrhage, eclampsia, and sepsis topping the list. The nurse midwife is often the first responder and must be trained to recognise, initiate first-line treatment, and mobilise the team rapidly for conditions such as cord prolapse, uterine rupture, and amniotic fluid embolism.
183.def:
Definition

Obstetric Emergency: Any acute, life-threatening condition occurring during pregnancy, labour, or the puerperium that requires immediate medical and nursing intervention to prevent maternal or fetal death; includes eclampsia, postpartum haemorrhage (PPH), cord prolapse, shoulder dystocia, amniotic fluid embolism (AFE), uterine rupture, and uterine inversion.

184.intro:
Introduction

Obstetric emergencies represent the most time-critical situations in midwifery practice, where a delay of even a few minutes can determine the difference between life and death for the mother and baby. India’s Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA) and LaQshya programmes specifically mandate nurse midwife training in emergency obstetric care (EmOC) to address the preventable maternal mortality burden. The nurse midwife’s role extends from early recognition and first-line stabilisation to documentation, communication, and post-event debriefing.
184.def:
Definition

Obstetric Emergency: A sudden, life-threatening complication arising during pregnancy, labour, or the puerperium requiring immediate skilled intervention; categorised as antepartum (APH, eclampsia, cord prolapse), intrapartum (shoulder dystocia, uterine rupture, AFE), and postpartum (PPH, uterine inversion, puerperal sepsis).

185.intro:
Introduction

The ability to enumerate and manage obstetric emergencies is a core clinical competency tested repeatedly in MUHS examinations, reflecting its critical importance in reducing India’s preventable maternal mortality. Cord prolapse, one of the most acute of all obstetric emergencies, demands a fetal head displaced off the cord within minutes to prevent irreversible hypoxic brain injury or death. The nurse midwife’s general responsibilities across all emergencies follow a universal framework: recognise, call for help, initiate first-line treatment, monitor, document, and refer.
185.def:
Definition

Cord Prolapse: Descent of the umbilical cord past the presenting part after rupture of membranes, resulting in cord compression between the presenting part and the maternal pelvis; a true obstetric emergency with a fetal mortality of up to 91% if not delivered within minutes. Cord presentation (cord felt through intact membranes) is the precursor state.

186.intro:
Introduction

Cord prolapse is among the most time-sensitive obstetric emergencies, with fetal prognosis directly dependent on the interval between diagnosis and delivery. In India, it most commonly occurs in association with malpresentation, polyhydramnios, and injudicious artificial rupture of membranes (ARM) with an unengaged head — situations frequently encountered in secondary care settings. The nurse midwife must be able to distinguish cord presentation (intact membranes) from cord prolapse (post-ROM), initiate the PUSH emergency sequence immediately, and facilitate emergency caesarean section or instrumental delivery without delay.

187.intro:
Introduction

Management of cord prolapse requires simultaneous execution of multiple emergency interventions, making it one of the most challenging yet teachable scenarios in obstetric nursing. The principle is simple: relieve cord compression by elevating the presenting part and expedite delivery. In Indian district hospitals and PHCs, the nurse midwife is often the only skilled birth attendant present at the moment of diagnosis and must act within seconds without waiting for a physician. Competency in the PUSH sequence is therefore non-negotiable for all trained nurse midwives in India.
187.def:
Definition

Cord Prolapse: Descent of the umbilical cord below or alongside the presenting part after rupture of membranes, leading to cord compression and acute fetal hypoxia; occult prolapse (cord lies alongside the presenting part, not visible) is detected by variable decelerations on CTG and confirmed on vaginal examination.

188.intro:
Introduction

Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is one of the rarest yet most catastrophic obstetric emergencies, with a reported mortality of 60–80% and no reliable method of prevention or prediction. It occurs when amniotic fluid, fetal cells, hair, or other debris enters the maternal pulmonary circulation, triggering an anaphylactoid response and catastrophic cardiorespiratory collapse followed by disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). The nurse midwife must recognise the classic triad immediately and initiate CPR & ICU transfer without delay, as survival is entirely dependent on speed of resuscitation.
188.def:
Definition

Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE): A rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency in which amniotic fluid, fetal squamous cells, lanugo, or vernix enter the maternal bloodstream through uterine or cervical veins, triggering an anaphylactoid reaction characterised by acute respiratory failure, cardiovascular collapse, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC); mortality 60–80%.

189.intro:
Introduction

Acute uterine inversion is a rare but life-threatening obstetric emergency occurring in the third stage of labour, predominantly due to mismanagement of the cord control (cord traction before placental separation or fundal pressure on an atonic uterus). It results in severe neurogenic and haemorrhagic shock and must be corrected within minutes to prevent maternal death. In India, where mismanaged third stage in home deliveries and under-resourced facilities remains a concern, the nurse midwife’s knowledge of prevention and immediate management is critical.
189.def:
Definition

Uterine Inversion: A rare obstetric emergency in which the uterine fundus collapses inward and turns inside out, protruding through the cervix into or beyond the vagina during the third stage of labour; classified as incomplete (fundus not beyond the cervix), complete (fundus beyond the cervix), or prolapsed (fundus visible at the vulva); most commonly caused by cord traction before placental separation.

190.intro:
Introduction

Fetal distress, now more precisely termed non-reassuring fetal status, reflects acute or chronic fetal hypoxia during labour and is one of the most common indications for emergency operative delivery in India. It is detected through continuous monitoring of the fetal heart rate (FHR) using a CTG or Pinard’s stethoscope, and by assessment of amniotic fluid colour. The nurse midwife must be able to recognise the hallmarks of fetal distress—bradycardia, late decelerations, and thick meconium—and immediately initiate intrauterine resuscitation while summoning obstetric support.
190.def:
Definition

Fetal Distress (Non-Reassuring Fetal Status): A clinical condition in which the fetus shows signs of inadequate oxygenation during pregnancy or labour, manifested as fetal heart rate <110 or >160 bpm, late decelerations, absent beat-to-beat variability on CTG, and/or meconium-stained amniotic fluid; requires immediate assessment and intervention to prevent fetal hypoxic injury or death.

191.intro:
Introduction

Injuries to the birth canal are common intrapartum complications that, if undetected or inadequately repaired, contribute to postpartum haemorrhage (trauma is one of the 4 T’s of PPH), long-term pelvic floor dysfunction, and anal incontinence. In India, where instrumental deliveries and deliveries without skilled attendance are more prevalent, birth canal injuries including cervical tears, vaginal lacerations, and third- and fourth-degree perineal tears remain a significant problem. The nurse midwife must systematically inspect the entire birth canal after every delivery and promptly repair or refer injuries as appropriate.
191.def:
Definition

Birth Canal Injuries: Traumatic lacerations or tears occurring to the cervix, vagina, perineum, or vulva during the second stage of labour; classified by location (cervical, vaginal, perineal) and severity; perineal tears are graded 1st–4th degree based on depth of tissue involvement, with 3rd degree (anal sphincter) and 4th degree (rectal mucosa) requiring specialist operative repair.

192.intro:
Introduction

Perineal tears are among the most frequently encountered complications of vaginal delivery, occurring in up to 85% of first-time mothers. While first- and second-degree tears are manageable at the bedside, third- and fourth-degree obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASIS) carry a risk of long-term anal incontinence and significantly impact quality of life. Prevention through controlled delivery of the fetal head and selective episiotomy, combined with meticulous repair and postoperative care, are key nursing responsibilities mandated under MUHS midwifery curriculum.
192.def:
Definition

Perineal Tear: A spontaneous laceration of the perineal body occurring during the second stage of labour, classified into four degrees: 1st degree (skin and vaginal mucosa only), 2nd degree (skin and perineal muscles, anal sphincter intact), 3rd degree (anal sphincter involved — 3a: <50%, 3b: >50%, 3c: internal anal sphincter), and 4th degree (sphincter plus rectal mucosa).

193.intro:
Introduction

Episiotomy—a deliberate surgical incision of the perineum to enlarge the vaginal outlet at delivery—was once performed routinely in India but is now recommended only on a selective basis by WHO (2018) and the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), as routine use has been shown to increase the risk of third- and fourth-degree tears. The nurse midwife must understand the current evidence-based indications, the correct technique for mediolateral incision, meticulous three-layer repair, and comprehensive postoperative perineal care to promote healing and prevent infection.
193.def:
Definition

Episiotomy: A deliberate, planned surgical incision of the perineum and posterior vaginal wall made during the second stage of labour at crowning to enlarge the vaginal outlet and facilitate delivery; performed under local anaesthesia (1% lignocaine) and repaired in three layers using absorbable sutures; types include mediolateral (45° from midline, most common in India) and median/midline.

194.intro:
Introduction

Induction of labour (IOL) is one of the most commonly performed obstetric interventions in India, accounting for approximately 20–25% of all deliveries in institutional settings, with rates rising due to increased detection of post-datism, gestational diabetes, and hypertensive disorders through enhanced antenatal surveillance. The nurse midwife’s role in IOL encompasses pre-induction assessment using the Bishop Score, safe administration of oxytocin under continuous CTG monitoring, early recognition of complications such as uterine hyperstimulation, and preparedness for emergency caesarean section in case of failed induction.
194.def:
Definition

Induction of Labour (IOL): The artificial stimulation of uterine contractions before the spontaneous onset of labour to achieve vaginal delivery; indicated when continuation of pregnancy poses greater risk to mother or fetus than delivery; requires a Bishop Score ≥6 (favourable cervix) for successful induction, or prior cervical ripening if Bishop Score <6.

195.intro:
Introduction

Ventouse (vacuum-assisted) delivery is an instrumental method used to expedite delivery during the second stage of labour when maternal pushing is insufficient or when rapid delivery is required for fetal or maternal indications. It is preferred over forceps in many Indian tertiary centres due to significantly lower rates of maternal perineal trauma, though it carries a unique risk of neonatal scalp injuries including cephalhaematoma and the potentially fatal subgaleal haemorrhage. The nurse midwife assists with preparation, monitoring, and neonatal assessment during and after ventouse delivery.
195.def:
Definition

Ventouse (Vacuum-Assisted) Delivery: An instrumental obstetric procedure in which a suction cup is applied to the fetal scalp at the flexion point (3 cm anterior to the posterior fontanelle) and negative pressure (0.6–0.8 kg/cm²) is created to allow traction and rotation during maternal expulsive efforts; requires full cervical dilation, engaged vertex presentation, and ruptured membranes.

196.intro:
Introduction

Forceps delivery is an instrumental obstetric procedure with a long history in midwifery, enabling assisted vaginal delivery when maternal effort alone is insufficient in the second stage or when urgent delivery is required to prevent maternal or fetal compromise. In India, forceps use has declined with the rise of vacuum extraction and increased caesarean rates, but remains an essential skill for complex situations such as aftercoming head in breech delivery and rotation of the persistent occipitoposterior position. The nurse midwife plays a critical preparatory and monitoring role during all forceps deliveries.
196.def:
Definition

Forceps Delivery: An instrumental obstetric procedure in which specially designed metal traction instruments are applied to the fetal head to assist delivery during the second stage of labour; types include outlet forceps (Wrigley’s — head visible), low forceps (Simpson’s — station +2 or below), and mid-cavity rotational forceps (Kielland’s — for OP rotation); requires full dilation, engaged cephalic presentation, and adequate analgesia.

197.intro:
Introduction

Caesarean section (C-section) is the most commonly performed major surgery in obstetrics worldwide and in India, where the national C-section rate has risen to approximately 21.5% (NFHS-5, 2019–21), well above the WHO-recommended 10–15%. While it is life-saving when indicated, unnecessary C-sections carry significant maternal risks including haemorrhage, infection, anaesthetic complications, and risks to future pregnancies. The nurse midwife must provide high-quality pre-operative preparation and systematic post-operative care to minimise complications and support early recovery and breastfeeding.
197.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section: A surgical procedure in which the fetus is delivered through incisions in the abdominal wall (laparotomy) and uterus (hysterotomy); the most common type is the Lower Segment Caesarean Section (LSCS), using a transverse incision on the lower uterine segment, which causes less blood loss, heals more securely, and allows subsequent vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC).

198.intro:
Introduction

Caesarean section is the most frequently performed major operative procedure in Indian obstetric units, with rates rising steeply in both public and private sectors. Understanding its types, indications, and perioperative nursing management is essential for the MUHS-trained nurse midwife who will be involved in pre-operative preparation, intraoperative scrub or circulating duties, and the full spectrum of post-operative recovery care. Recognition and early management of post-caesarean complications—including wound infection, paralytic ileus, and deep vein thrombosis—are equally important nursing competencies.
198.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section: Delivery of the fetus via surgical incision through the abdominal wall and uterus; performed as elective (planned, non-emergency), emergency (immediate threat to mother or fetus), or urgent (compromise not immediately life-threatening); the LSCS (lower segment transverse incision) accounts for 95% of all caesarean sections and is the preferred type in contemporary practice.

199.intro:
Introduction

India’s caesarean section rate has more than doubled over the past two decades, rising from 8.5% in NFHS-3 (2005–06) to 21.5% in NFHS-5 (2019–21), with private hospitals recording rates as high as 47.4%. This far exceeds the WHO-recommended ceiling of 10–15%, raising concerns about unnecessary surgical risk, increased healthcare costs, and implications for future pregnancies including placenta accreta spectrum. The nurse midwife plays an important advocacy and preparatory role, supporting VBAC where appropriate, counselling women against unnecessary C-sections, and ensuring meticulous perioperative care when surgery is genuinely indicated.
199.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section Rate: The proportion of all live births delivered by caesarean section in a given population; the WHO (2015) recommends a population-level C-section rate of 10–15%, below which maternal and perinatal mortality decreases with increasing caesarean availability, and above which no further mortality benefit is demonstrated but risks of surgical morbidity increase.

200.intro:
Introduction

The indications for caesarean section encompass a broad spectrum of maternal, fetal, and placental conditions, and their correct identification is one of the most important clinical decision-making competencies in obstetric nursing. In India, understanding both absolute indications (where vaginal delivery is impossible or unsafe) and relative indications (where clinical judgement weighs risks and benefits) is essential for the nurse midwife who must assist obstetricians in decision-making, prepare families for emergency procedures, and contribute to audit and quality improvement in reducing unnecessary caesarean rates.
200.def:
Definition

Indications for Caesarean Section: Clinical conditions in which caesarean delivery is necessary or preferable to vaginal delivery to protect maternal or fetal health; classified as absolute (vaginal delivery impossible or life-threatening — e.g., major placenta praevia, cord prolapse, severe CPD, transverse lie at term) or relative (C-section preferable given the clinical context — e.g., fetal distress, breech, failed induction, previous classical scar).
